moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the London System, one of the most popular and practical openings for White. White plays 1.d4, claiming central space and preparing the Bf4 development that defines the London. The London leads to solid, strategic positions where White builds a resilient pawn structure and avoids sharp theoretical battles.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single central pawn on d4. White aims to build a solid d4-e3 pawn duo rather than an aggressive d4.
plans: Develop the dark-squared bishop to f4 before playing e3
Build a solid pawn triangle with d4-e3-c3
alternatives: Nf3 — a flexible move that can also lead to the London after Bf4
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.d4 and Bf4 define the London System — solid, strategic, avoiding sharp theory.

moveAnnotations.annotation: In the London System, the King's Indian Counterattack is a dangerous line you must be aware of.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: London System's King's Indian Counterattack threatens a quick kingside attack with ...Nf6, ...g6, ...Bg7, and ...0-0.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Opening with 1.d4 as always in the London. But in this move order — popularized by GothamChess and Hikaru — we delay Bf4 and play Nf3 first.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.d4, delaying Bf4, plays Nf3 first in the London.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black takes the classical approach with 1...d5, establishing central control. This is the most common reply and leads to the standard London structure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1...d5 establishes central control, leading to a standard London structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Here's the key difference from the main line: 2.Nf3 before Bf4. This prevents Black from playing an early ...Bf5 or ...c5 with tempo against our bishop.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 2.Nf3 before Bf4 prevents Black's early ...Bf5 or ...c5 with tempo.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the kingside knight, controlling e4 and preparing to castle. A natural and solid response.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight develops to control e4 and prepares kingside castling.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Now we play Bf4, the London signature move. By waiting until move 3, we've ensured Black has already committed their knight to f6, making it harder for them to harass our bishop with ...Qb6 or grab.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bf4 develops the bishop to f6, avoiding harassment from ...Qb6 or ...grab.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at our d4 pawn with ...c5, the main way to challenge the London center. This is the most principled response, creating tension immediately.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...c5 challenges the d4 pawn, creating immediate central tension.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We solidify the center with e3, forming the classic London pawn triangle. The d4 pawn is now well-supported, and we've opened the diagonal for the light-squared bishop.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e3 solidifies d4 with the London pawn triangle and opens the light-squared bishop's diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the queenside knight to its ideal square, adding pressure to d4 and preparing to complete development.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight on c6 pressures d4 and prepares to finish development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop the knight to d2 rather than c3. This is a typical London choice — the knight on d2 supports e4 pushes and keeps the c-pawn free to play c3 to reinforce d4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to d2 supports e4 and keeps c3 open to reinforce d4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays a solid ...e6, bolstering d5 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop. The position is taking on a classical Queen's Gambit flavor.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...e6 bolsters d5 and prepares the dark-squared bishop, resembling a Queen's Gambit structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Completing the London pawn triangle: d4-e3-c3. Our center is rock solid. This is the ideal structure — hard for Black to break down and easy for us to play behind.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: The London pawn triangle d4-e3-c3 forms a rock-solid center hard for Black to break.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to d6, directly challenging our Bf4. This is the critical moment — we need to decide whether to exchange bishops or retreat to g3.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Bd6 challenges our Bf4 — we must exchange or retreat to g3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We retreat to g3 rather than trading. The bishop on g3 is still active on the h2-b8 diagonal and prevents Black from getting the bishop pair.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Retreating to g3 keeps the bishop active on h2-b8 and denies Black the bishop pair.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles, getting the king to safety. The position is balanced but White has a very clear plan: Bd3, O-O, and then look for e4 or a kingside buildup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black castles; White plans Bd3, O-O, then e4 or a kingside buildup.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Developing the bishop to its ideal square. From d3 it aims at h7, creating a potential battery with the queen. This is the classic London attacking setup against a castled king.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop to d3 aims at h7, setting up a queen battery against the castled king.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black prepares to fianchetto the light-squared bishop to b7, where it will pressure e4 and the long diagonal. A solid queenside development plan.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black fianchettoes the light-squared bishop to b7, pressuring e4 and the long diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We castle and our setup is complete. The position is comfortable for White: solid center, well-placed pieces, and clear plans. We can aim for e4, Qe2 + e4, or build up on the kingside with Ne5 and f4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White is comfortable with a solid center and clear kingside plans like e4, Qe2, or Ne5 with f4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto. The bishop on b7 is well-placed on the long diagonal. This is the typical middlegame structure — both sides are fully developed and the strategic battle begins.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's fianchettoed bishop on b7 eyes the long diagonal as both sides finish development and the middlegame begins.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Starting with d4 as usual. This line covers what to do when Black challenges us with an early ...Qb6, targeting b2. Hikaru's specific recommendation for handling this annoying line.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: After d4, Black's early Qb6 targets b2 — Hikaru recommends defending with a3 and developing naturally.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays the standard 1...d5. So far nothing unusual — the fireworks come later.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black plays 1...d5, the Scandinavian Defense, with fireworks to follow.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We use the modern Nf3-first move order again. This line works whether you play Nf3 or Bf4 first, but the Nf3 order is slightly more accurate.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nf3 first is slightly more accurate than Bf4 in this line.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight naturally. The ...Qb6 idea is coming soon.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight develops, threatening Qb6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play our London bishop move. Black will now try to exploit the fact that this bishop has left b2 undefended.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: The London bishop leaves b2 undefended, and Black will target it.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at the center with ...c5 first, preparing to bring the queen out with tempo.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...c5 strikes the center, preparing Qa5 with tempo.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We calmly reinforce d4. The b2 pawn looks weak, but we have a plan for that.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Reinforce d4; the b2 pawn is weak but part of a plan.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight, adding pressure to d4 and preparing the queen sortie.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight develops to pressure d4 and prepare a queen sortie.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play c3 to shore up d4. This invites Black to play ...Qb6, which looks scary but we're ready for it.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: c3 shores up d4, inviting ...Qb6 but White is ready.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Here it comes! Black attacks b2 with the queen. Many London players panic here, but we have a clean solution. Don't try to defend b2 passively — we counter-attack.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's queen attacks b2 — counter-attack instead of defending passively.

moveAnnotations.annotation: the critical move! We meet the queen with our own queen, offering a trade. If Black takes on b3, we recapture with axb3 and our rook opens on the a-file — great for us. Most players will decline the trade.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Queen trade on b3 opens the a-file for your rook if Black captures.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black pushes ...c4, chasing our queen away and grabbing queenside space. This looks aggressive but it actually helps us — Black's queen is now misplaced on b6 and the c4 pawn can become a target lat.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...c4 chases the queen but leaves the c4 pawn weak and the queen misplaced on b6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We reroute the queen to c2, a perfect square. From here the queen eyes h7 (once Bd3 comes) and stays connected to the queenside. The queen is much better here than it was on d1.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Queen reroutes to c2, eyeing h7 after Bd3 and staying connected to the queenside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop actively to f5, trying to challenge our queen's diagonal. This is Black's most natural development.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the bishop to f5, challenging the queen's diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We drop the queen back to c1. It might look odd, but from c1 the queen connects to the Bf4, supports a future Nh4 hitting the Bf5, and keeps flexible options.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Queen drops to c1, connecting to Bf4 and supporting Nh4 against Bf5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black solidifies with ...e6, supporting d5 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop. The position is balanced but White has a clear plan.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...e6 supports d5 and develops the dark-squared bishop, but White has a clear plan.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Developing the knight to d2. From here it can go to e4 or help with the e4 pawn break. Notice how despite the early queen adventures, our development is excellent.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to d2 eyes e4 and supports the e4 pawn break — development is excellent despite early queen moves.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop modestly to e7. The queen on b6 is now looking a bit misplaced — it's not doing much and will need to find a better home soon.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's queen on b6 is misplaced and will need to find a better square.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Classical London setup — straightforward and easy to remember. This is Anna Cramling's recommended line: just nine half-moves that give you a complete, solid position.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Classical London setup: nine half-moves yield a solid position with no early weaknesses.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays the symmetrical 1...d5. This Classical line works best against this response, giving White a natural development scheme.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's 1...d5 invites the Classical line, giving White natural development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: In the Classical version, we play Bf4 on move 2 — the traditional London move order. Get the bishop out before it gets locked behind the e3 pawn.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bf4 on move 2 develops the bishop before it gets locked behind e3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, the ideal square. It controls e4 and prepares kingside castling. Nothing surprising here.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight on f6 controls e4 and prepares kingside castling.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We lock in the pawn structure with e3. The bishop is already on f4, so we're not blocking anything.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e3 locks the pawn structure without blocking the f4-bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors with ...e6, creating a solid pawn duo on d5 and e6. This is the most classical setup — both sides have secure, symmetrical centers.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's e6 mirrors White's e4, forming a solid d5–e6 pawn duo with symmetrical, secure centers.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We bring out the knight to f3, controlling e5 and d4. The knight and bishop work together to dominate the dark squares in the center. Three pieces developed and we're ready to castle.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to f3 controls e5 and d4, dominating dark squares with the bishop — three pieces developed, ready to castle.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a modest but solid square. It prepares castling and doesn't commit to any aggressive plan yet.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the bishop to e7, preparing castling without committing to an aggressive plan.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The final piece of the Classical setup! The bishop on d3 aims at h7, creating attacking potential against a castled king.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop to d3 aims at h7, threatening a kingside attack against a castled king.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Opening with 1.d4. In this Carlsen-inspired move order, Black responds with 1...Nf6 instead of the usual 1...d5, but we proceed with our standard London setup regardless.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.d4 Nf6 — White plays the London System regardless.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays 1...Nf6, an Indian Defense move order. This delays ...d5 and keeps options open for a King's Indian or Nimzo-Indian setup. We stick to our London plan.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black plays 1...Nf6, an Indian Defense, delaying ...d5; White continues with the London setup.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Developing the knight first, keeping the London flexible. This prevents Black from playing ...e5 with tempo and prepares Bf4 on the next move.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Developing the knight first prevents ...e5 with tempo and prepares Bf4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays ...d5, transposing toward a standard London structure. Now the center is symmetrical and we continue with our setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...d5 transposes to a symmetrical London structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The London bishop comes out to f4 before e3 locks it in. This is the defining move of the London System.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: London bishop develops to f4 before e3 locks it in.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black challenges the d4 pawn immediately with ...c5. This is the most principled response, creating central tension before White can lock things down.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black challenges d4 with ...c5, creating immediate central tension.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Reinforcing d4 and completing the pawn chain. The bishop is already out on f4, so e3 doesn't trap it. We now have the classic London pawn triangle forming.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e3 reinforces d4 and completes the London pawn triangle with the bishop already on f4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to c6, adding pressure to d4. A natural and standard developing choice in this structure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight to c6 adds pressure to d4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Developing the knight to d2 rather than c3. This keeps the c-pawn free for c3, supporting d4, and the knight can reroute to e5 or support e4 later.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to d2 keeps c-pawn free for c3 to support d4, with reroutes to e5 or e4 later.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black solidifies the d5 pawn with ...e6 and prepares to develop the dark-squared bishop. A solid, classical approach.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black solidifies d5 with e6, preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Completing the pawn triangle with d4-e3-c3. The center is now rock solid and very hard for Black to crack open. This is the ideal London structure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: The d4-e3-c3 pawn triangle locks the center, making it very hard for Black to crack open.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to d6, directly challenging our Bf4. This forces us to make a decision about the dark-squared bishops.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Bd6 challenges our Bf4, forcing a trade or retreat on the dark squares.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Retreating to g3 rather than trading. The bishop stays active on the diagonal and if Black trades on g3, we recapture with hxg3, opening the h-file for attack.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Retreating to g3 keeps the bishop active; if Black trades on g3, recapture hxg3 opens the h-file.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside, getting the king to safety. Now we complete our development with Bd3 and O-O.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black castles kingside; White develops Bd3 and O-O.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop goes to its ideal square on d3, aiming at h7 and creating potential for a kingside battery with the queen. Classic London attacking setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop to d3 aims at h7, setting up a queen-battery kingside attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black prepares to fianchetto the light-squared bishop to b7, pressuring the long diagonal. A natural plan in this structure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black fianchettoes the light-squared bishop to b7, pressuring the long diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We castle and the setup is complete. All pieces are on ideal squares and we have a clear plan heading into the middlegame.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Castled with all pieces on ideal squares, the middlegame plan is clear.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto. The bishop on b7 pressures e4 and the long diagonal. Both sides are fully developed.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's fianchettoed bishop on b7 pressures e4 and the long diagonal; both sides are fully developed.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The key outpost move! Carlsen's favorite in this structure. The knight on e5 is a monster — it attacks c6, d7, f7, and supports a kingside attack.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to e5 attacks c6, d7, f7, and supports a kingside attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Starting with 1.d4 as always. This line covers Hikaru's specific handling of Black's aggressive ...Qb6 response, which targets the b2 pawn after Bf4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.d4, then Black's ...Qb6 targets b2 after Bf4 — White must defend the pawn.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays the standard 1...d5, establishing central presence. The ...Qb6 idea is coming soon.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's 1...d5 claims the center, setting up ...Qb6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Developing the knight first with the modern move order. This keeps things flexible and prepares Bf4 on the next move.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Developing the knight first keeps flexibility and prepares Bf4 next.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the kingside knight naturally, controlling e4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight develops to control e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The London bishop comes out to f4. Black will now look to exploit the fact that b2 is undefended with an early ...Qb6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: London bishop to f4 leaves b2 undefended, Black targets it with ...Qb6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at d4 with ...c5, preparing to follow up with ...Qb6 to attack b2 with tempo.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...c5 attacks d4, setting up ...Qb6 to target b2 with tempo.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Reinforcing d4 with e3. The b2 pawn looks vulnerable but we have a concrete plan to deal with ...Qb6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Reinforcing d4 with e3; b2 looks loose but we have a concrete plan against ...Qb6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to c6, adding pressure to d4 and preparing the queen sortie.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight to c6 pressures d4 and prepares a queen sortie.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play c3, completing the pawn triangle and inviting Black to try ...Qb6. We're fully prepared for it.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: c3 completes the pawn triangle, inviting Black's Qb6 — White is fully prepared.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays the aggressive ...Qb6, targeting b2. This looks threatening but Hikaru has a clean antidote.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...Qb6 targets b2, but White refutes it with a clean antidote.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The key response! We counter with Qb3, offering a queen trade. If Black takes on b3, axb3 opens the a-file for our rook. Most players decline.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Qb3 offers a queen trade; if Black declines, the queen eyes b7 and pressures the queenside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black pushes ...c4, chasing the queen and grabbing queenside space. This looks aggressive but it actually helps White — the c4 pawn overextends and the queen on b6 becomes misplaced.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...c4 overextends the pawn and misplaces the queen on b6, handing White the initiative.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We reroute to c2, an excellent square. The queen eyes h7 for future attacks and stays flexible. This is the first step of Hikaru's Qb3-Qc2-Qc1 maneuver.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Queen reroutes to c2, eyeing h7 and staying flexible for the Qb3-Qc2-Qc1 maneuver.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop actively to f5, challenging our queen's diagonal and trying to seize the initiative.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's bishop to f5 challenges the queen's diagonal and seizes the initiative.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The queen drops back to c1, completing the maneuver. From c1 it connects to the Bf4, supports a future Nh4 hitting Bf5, and keeps all options open. Black's queenside expansion has been neutralized.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Queen retreats to c1, connecting to Bf4 and supporting Nh4 against Bf5, neutralizing Black's queenside expansion.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black solidifies with ...e6, supporting d5 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop. The position is stabilizing.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e6 supports d5 and prepares the dark-squared bishop's development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Developing the knight to d2. It can head to e4 or support a future e4 push. Despite the early queen adventures, White's development is harmonious.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to d2 eyes e4 and supports a future e4 push — White's development is harmonious.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop modestly to e7, preparing to castle. The queen on b6 is looking increasingly misplaced.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's queen on b6 is misplaced after bishop e7 prepares kingside castling.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop the bishop to e2 rather than d3, since the Bf5 controls the d3 square. The bishop on e2 is solid and supports a future kingside expansion.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop to e2 avoids Bf5's control of d3 and supports kingside expansion.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles, getting the king to safety. The opening phase is nearly complete.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black castles king to safety, completing the opening phase.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We castle as well. White's position is harmonious with clear plans: Nh4 to trade the Bf5, then push e4 to open the center.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Castle, then Nh4 trades the Bf5 and e4 opens the center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black reroutes the knight, likely heading for b6 or f8. This is the resulting position from Hikaru's Anti-Qb6 system.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight reroutes to b6 or f8 in Hikaru's Anti-Qb6 system.

moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the London System, one of the most popular and practical openings for White. White plays 1.d4, claiming central space and preparing the Bf4 development that defines the London. The London leads to solid, strategic positions where White builds a resilient pawn structure and avoids sharp theoretical battles.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single central pawn on d4. White aims to build a solid d4-e3 pawn duo rather than an aggressive d4.
plans: Develop the dark-squared bishop to f4 before playing e3
Build a solid pawn triangle with d4-e3-c3
alternatives: Nf3 — a flexible move that can also lead to the London after Bf4
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.d4 and Bf4 define the London System — solid, strategic, avoiding sharp theory.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds with 1...d5, establishing symmetrical central control. The d5 pawn controls c4 and e4, matching White's claim. In the London, this leads to a closed, maneuvering game where pawn structure and piece placement matter more than tactical fireworks.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5 central pawns. Neither side has committed to a pawn exchange yet, maintaining.
plans: Maintain the d4-d5 tension rather than exchanging
Develop the bishop to f4 before locking it in with e3
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1...d5 matches central control, leading to a closed London where pawn structure and piece placement outweigh tactics.

moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the signature move of the London System — White develops the dark-squared bishop to f4 BEFORE playing e3. This move order is critical because after e3, the bishop would be locked behind the pawn chain with no good diagonal. The Bf4 controls the h2-b8 diagonal, eyes the e5 square, and is the foundation of the entire London setup.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Still d4 vs d5. The key principle: always develop Bf4 before playing e3, or the bishop gets buried.
plans: Support e5 control and prevent Black from freeing their position with ...e5
After e3-Bd3, aim the bishop battery at Black's kingside
alternatives: Nf3 — also good, but risks Black playing ...Bf5 to contest the diagonal
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bf4 before e3 keeps the bishop active on the h2-b8 diagonal, controlling e5 and avoiding the pawn chain.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, attacking the e4 square and preparing kingside castling. The Nf6 is the most flexible response — it develops a piece, pressures the center, and waits to see White's exact setup before committing to a pawn structure.

plans: Continue development with ...e6 and ...Be7 or ...Bd6

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays e3, creating the London's trademark pawn triangle on d4-e3-c3 (c3 comes later). The e3 pawn supports d4, opens the f1-a6 diagonal for the bishop to develop to d3, and creates a rock-solid center. The cost is locking in the dark-squared bishop — which is why Bf4 was played first.
